title: Concluding the Sāmāyika (Āvaśyakas 5 & 6)
summary: This step finishes the fifth Āvaśyaka (kāyotsarga) with a five-Logassa standing meditation, takes the sixth Āvaśyaka (the cauvihāra pratyākhyāna / renunciation vow) through the guru's ādeśa, recounts all six Āvaśyakas, and then performs the formal procedure for ending the 48-minute sāmāyika.

segments.recitation: Dukkhakkhaya kammakkhaya-nimittaṁ karemi kāussaggaṁ — Annattha — (five Logassa) ... siddhā siddhiṁ mama disantu
segments.plainMeaning: Stand in silent meditation for five Logassa to wear away suffering and karma.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: Saying "For the destruction of suffering and the destruction of karma I undertake the kāyotsarga," then reciting "Annattha," perform a full kāyotsarga of five complete Logassa, meditating up to "siddhā siddhiṁ mama disantu."
segments.explanation: The standing kāyotsarga is measured here as five silent Logassa, ending at the closing line "siddhā siddhiṁ mama disantu."

ritualTerms.gloss: standing motionless body-abandonment meditation
the formula naming permitted exceptions that do not break the kāyotsarga
the hymn to the 24 Tīrthaṅkaras, used here as the unit counting the meditation

segments.plainMeaning: Recite Logassa aloud, completing the fifth obligation.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: Then recite the Logassa aloud. With this the fifth Āvaśyaka, called kāyotsarga, is complete; by performing it any transgressions still remaining are also purified.
segments.explanation: The silent kāyotsarga is now 'released' by saying Logassa aloud, marking the close of the fifth Āvaśyaka.

ritualTerms.gloss: one of the six daily obligatory duties
a transgression or fault against the vows

segments.recitation: (offer one khamāsamaṇa, then stand and take the ādeśa)
segments.plainMeaning: Bow once, stand, and ask the guru's permission.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: Then offer one khamāsamaṇa (prostration of respect), rise to your feet, and take the ādeśa (the guru's leave to proceed).
segments.explanation: A procedural cue that sets up the request that follows in the next segment.

ritualTerms.gloss: a reverential prostration asking forgiveness of the guru
the guru's formal permission to proceed

segments.recitation: Icchākāreṇa saṁdisaha bhagavan! Chaṭṭhā āvaśyaka bhaṇī pacchakkhāṇa vāndaṇā dauṁjī?
segments.plainMeaning: The disciple asks leave to offer the vāndaṇā for the sixth obligation, the vow of renunciation.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: "If it be your wish, command me, O Reverend One! For the sixth Āvaśyaka, the pratyākhyāna, may I offer the vāndaṇā (guru-veneration)?"
segments.explanation: The disciple's opening request introducing the twelve-gesture vāndaṇā before the sixth Āvaśyaka.

ritualTerms.gloss: the twelve-gesture formal veneration of the guru (Āv. 3)
the vow of renunciation, the sixth Āvaśyaka

segments.plainMeaning: The guru replies, "Offer it."
segments.idiomaticTranslation: (The guru answers) "Offer it."
segments.explanation: The guru's brief grant of permission in response to the disciple's request.

segments.plainMeaning: The disciple accepts, saying "As you wish."
segments.idiomaticTranslation: (The disciple responds) "Icchaṁ — as you wish, I accept."
segments.explanation: The standard formula of acceptance following the guru's command.

segments.plainMeaning: Perform the two vāndaṇā.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: Offer the two vāndaṇā (the twelve-gesture guru-veneration).
segments.explanation: Directs the actual performance of the two venerations requested above.

ritualTerms.gloss: the twelve-gesture formal veneration of the guru

segments.recitation: Icchakārī bhagavan! Pasāo karī, pacchakkhāṇanī āśa karāvojī
segments.plainMeaning: The disciple asks the guru to graciously administer the renunciation vow.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: "O gracious Reverend One! Bestowing your favor, kindly have me take the pratyākhyāna (the vow of renunciation)."
segments.explanation: The disciple's request that the guru lead him in formally taking the pratyākhyāna.

ritualTerms.gloss: the vow of renunciation for the coming period

segments.plainMeaning: Take the four-fold renunciation vow from the guru, or, failing that, on your own.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: Take the cauvihāra (four-fold) pratyākhyāna from the guru's own mouth; in his absence, from a knowledgeable elder; and failing even that, take the cauvihāra pratyākhyāna by yourself. With this pratyākhyāna the sixth Āvaśyaka is complete, and by performing it the purity of renunciation is attained.
segments.explanation: Gives the order of preference for who administers the vow and marks the completion of the sixth Āvaśyaka.

ritualTerms.gloss: the four-fold renunciation (abstaining from all four kinds of food and drink)
one of the six daily obligatory duties

segments.plainMeaning: Stand up to make the next declaration.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: Then, standing, recite the following.
segments.explanation: A posture cue introducing the recounting of the six Āvaśyakas.

segments.recitation: Pahele sāmāyika 1, bīje cauvisattho 2, trīje vāndaṇā 3, cothe paḍikkamaṇuṁ 4, pāṁcame kāusagga 5, chaṭṭhe pacchakkhāṇa 6 — e cha āvaśyaka pūrā thayā.
segments.plainMeaning: The disciple names all six obligations in order, declaring them complete.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: "In the first, Sāmāyika (1); in the second, Cauvīsattho (2); in the third, Vāndaṇā (3); in the fourth, Paḍikkamaṇa (4); in the fifth, Kāussagga (5); in the sixth, Pacchakkhāṇa (6) — these six Āvaśyakas are now complete."
segments.explanation: A formal recitation enumerating the six Āvaśyakas in their fixed order.

ritualTerms.gloss: the vow of equanimity (Āv. 1)
praise of the 24 Tīrthaṅkaras (Āv. 2)
veneration of the guru (Āv. 3)
reflection and atonement (Āv. 4)
standing meditation (Āv. 5)
renunciation vow (Āv. 6)

segments.plainMeaning: Mentally review the six obligations just completed.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: In this manner, call to mind and recount the six Āvaśyakas.
segments.explanation: A reflective cue to recollect the six duties as a whole.

ritualTerms.gloss: the six daily obligatory duties

segments.plainMeaning: This is the procedure for ending the sāmāyika.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: The procedure for concluding the sāmāyika.
segments.explanation: A heading announcing the closing rite of the 48-minute sāmāyika.

ritualTerms.gloss: the timed vow of equanimity being concluded
the prescribed ritual procedure

segments.plainMeaning: Bow once before the next request.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: Then offer one khamāsamaṇa (prostration).
segments.explanation: Sets up the iriyāvahiyaṁ request that follows.

ritualTerms.gloss: a reverential prostration to the guru

segments.recitation: Icchākāreṇa saṁdisaha bhagavan! Sacittane saṁghaṭṭe iriyāvahiyaṁ paḍikkamuṁjī?
segments.plainMeaning: The disciple asks leave to atone for any contact with living beings by reciting Iriyāvahiyaṁ.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: "If it be your wish, command me, O Reverend One! For any contact with living (sacitta) things, may I perform the iriyāvahiyaṁ (confession of harm caused while moving)?"
segments.explanation: The disciple's request to make the iriyāvahiyaṁ confession before leaving the sāmāyika.

ritualTerms.gloss: things possessing life
the confession (micchāmi dukkaḍaṁ) for harm to living beings while moving

segments.recitation: (iriyāvahiyaṁ; one Logassa kāyotsarga; then Logassa aloud)
segments.plainMeaning: Perform Iriyāvahiyaṁ, then a one-Logassa meditation, and recite Logassa aloud.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: Having taken the ādeśa, perform the 'iriyāvahiyaṁ,' do a kāyotsarga of one Logassa, and then recite the Logassa aloud.
segments.explanation: Specifies the single-Logassa measure of this kāyotsarga, released by saying Logassa aloud.

ritualTerms.gloss: the confession for harm to living beings while moving
standing motionless meditation
the hymn to the 24 Tīrthaṅkaras, the unit of the meditation

segments.recitation: (offer one khamāsamaṇa, stand, take the ādeśa)
segments.plainMeaning: Bow once, stand, and ask permission again.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: Then offer one khamāsamaṇa, rise to your feet, and take the ādeśa.
segments.explanation: A cue preparing the request to conclude the sāmāyika.

ritualTerms.gloss: a reverential prostration to the guru
the guru's permission to proceed

segments.plainMeaning: The disciple asks leave to conclude the sāmāyika.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: "If it be your wish, command me, O Reverend One! May I now conclude the sāmāyika?"
segments.explanation: The formal request seeking permission to end the timed vow.

ritualTerms.gloss: the timed vow of equanimity

segments.plainMeaning: The guru replies that it should be done again.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: (The guru answers) "It is to be done once more" — i.e., this practice should be taken up again.
segments.explanation: The guru's response encouraging repetition of the sāmāyika.

segments.plainMeaning: The disciple replies, "As far as I am able."
segments.idiomaticTranslation: (The disciple responds) "Yathāśakti — according to my capacity."
segments.explanation: The disciple's humble assent to undertake the practice again as ability permits.

segments.recitation: (again offer a khamāsamaṇa, stand, and say)
segments.plainMeaning: Bow once more, stand, and make the next declaration.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: Once again offer a khamāsamaṇa, rise to your feet, and recite the following.
segments.explanation: A cue introducing the confirmation that the sāmāyika has been concluded.

ritualTerms.gloss: a reverential prostration to the guru

segments.plainMeaning: The disciple asks whether the sāmāyika has been duly concluded.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: "If it be your wish, command me, O Reverend One! Has the sāmāyika been concluded?"
segments.explanation: The disciple seeks the guru's confirmation that the vow is now ended.

ritualTerms.gloss: the timed vow of equanimity

segments.plainMeaning: The guru replies that it is accomplished and right conduct must not be forsaken.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: (The guru answers) "It has been duly accomplished; the right conduct (ācāra) is not to be abandoned."
segments.explanation: The guru affirms completion while charging the disciple to uphold proper conduct.

ritualTerms.gloss: right conduct or observance

segments.plainMeaning: The disciple assents, saying "So be it."
segments.idiomaticTranslation: (The disciple responds) "Tahatti — it is so; so be it."
segments.explanation: The disciple's formula of glad acceptance of the guru's words.

segments.recitation: (offer one khamāsamaṇa, sit in the squatting posture, take the ādeśa)
segments.plainMeaning: Bow once, sit in the squatting posture, and ask permission.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: Offer one khamāsamaṇa, then sit down low in the ubhaḍaka (squatting on the haunches) posture, and take the ādeśa.
segments.explanation: Notes the particular squatting posture (ubhaḍaka āsana) adopted for the final ādeśa.

ritualTerms.gloss: a reverential prostration to the guru
the low squatting posture seated on the haunches
the guru's permission to proceed

segments.plainMeaning: The disciple again seeks the guru's leave, "Command me, O Reverend One."
segments.idiomaticTranslation: "If it be your wish, command me, O Reverend One!"
segments.explanation: The opening ādeśa formula closing this step, leading into what follows in the rite.

notes: Translations follow the native Gujarati/Prakrit verbatim with no added steps or counts. The phrase 'pacchakkhāṇanī āśa karāvojī' (seg 08) is read as the disciple asking the guru to administer/lead the pratyākhyāna; 'āśa' is taken as the intent/resolve to take the vow — rendered idiomatically rather than literally. 'Deha' (seg 05) is read as the imperative 'give/offer (it).' Guru/disciple responses (puṇo vi kāyavvaṁ, ārāhiyaṁ āyāro na muttavvo) follow standard sāmāyika-pāravā exchanges and are consistent with the KB's description of ādeśa call-and-response, though the book's exact wording for these specific lines was not independently re-verified here. No facts beyond the supplied KB and native text were invented.
